one_line_takeaway: A single low-privilege malicious agent that merely disguises a URL's structure (especially by registering a fresh domain) can push a malicious link through LLM multi-agent systems with 57.6% average success, bypassing all traditional and LLM-prompt defenses tested.
key_finding: WFA is broadly effective and evades every tested defense. Overall ASR is 57.6%, with the best single variant (DNR, newly registered domain) reaching 80% and clearing >=50% ASR in 82.8% of all model/architecture/defense configurations. Traditional URL-reputation defenses are essentially useless against structurally novel links, and LLM-prompt defenses only partially help (best DO 53.7% ASR). The most vulnerable architecture is Debate (88.5% ASR) because repeated debate rounds let the malicious agent reinforce legitimacy; the safest is Vote (37.1%) because many honest voters dilute the single malicious agent's influence. DNR and manipulation-based variants (SNM/PM/DM) work best because newly registered/structurally clean domains lack blacklist and training-data cues, exposing a systemic LLM blind spot in handling structured URL fields.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Web Fraud Attacks (WFA), a novel class of attacks against LLM-driven multi-agent systems (MAS) in which a single low-privilege malicious agent recommends a malicious URL whose structure is manipulated so that the MAS treats it as benign and forwards it to the user. The authors decompose a URL into a 5-tuple (top-level domain D_TL, second-level domain D_SL, subdomain D_S, path P, parameter Pa) and design 12 concrete attack variants grouped under 5 structural-manipulation strategies: (1) reducing malicious semantic features via IP obfuscation (IO) and domain-name registration (DNR); (2) registering a new D_SL maximizing similarity to a benign domain via typo insertion (TI), typo substitution (TS), typo repetition (TR), and homograph attack (HA); (3) subdomain manipulation (SNM) and subdomain imitation (SI); (4) directory manipulation (DM) and directory imitation (DI); and (5) parameter manipulation (PM) and parameter imitation (PI). The key claimed novelty over prior MAS attacks is a WEAKER threat model: WFA needs only one low-privilege compromised agent, no complex prompt engineering, and no knowledge of defenses or architecture, yet still evades detection. NOTE: attacks such as Chained Compromise, Consensus Forgery, PeerGuard backdoors, AgentPoison, Prompt Virus, and Information Worm belong to the Related Work section and are cited comparisons, NOT this paper's contribution.
threat_model: Black-box attack. The attacker controls exactly ONE malicious agent, which occupies the LOWEST-privilege position in the MAS (e.g., a basic travel assistant), and cannot see other agents' capabilities, the deployed defenses, or the MAS architecture; it interacts only over fixed channels defined by the MAS builder. The attacker's goal is to have its malicious link accepted through the MAS workflow and ultimately recommended to the user (or auto-visited by tool-using LLMs). Explicitly weaker than prior work: prior papers assume multiple compromised agents or high-privilege compromised agents; WFA assumes a single, lowest-position agent that merely provides a structurally disguised URL and insists on it if challenged during review/debate.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (1) threat model is confined to a single low-privilege malicious agent; colluding agents, compromised high-privilege agents (auditors/experts), and tool-augmented agents are not explored; (2) proposed mitigations (DNS/WHOIS traceability, LLM fine-tuning on adversarial links, whitelisting, DSL-only extraction) are preliminary and NOT empirically validated; (3) ethical constraints prevented real-world experiments with live users or live websites. Additional reviewer-observed limitations: the success criterion is a single 'auditor' emitting a risk label (a somewhat artificial proxy for real user harm); experiments run on one MAS framework (MetaGPT) with a fixed travel-recommendation scenario; per-cell ASRs are estimated from only 10 repetitions, producing very coarse (often 0%/100%) values; and there is no comparison against a prior attack baseline (WFA is measured in absolute ASR, not relative to existing MAS attacks).

datasets_used: No standard public dataset; authors construct malicious/benign URL pairs by hand (benign anchors such as google.com) and instantiate 12 WFA variants against a travel-website-recommendation task built on MetaGPT
Evaluation LLMs: Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-Reasoner, Llama3-8B
MAS platform: MetaGPT with 4 custom architectures (Linear, Review, Debate, Vote)
quantitative_results: Attack variants ASR: DNR 80% (highest), PM 69%, DM 68%, SNM 64%, TS 56%, TI 55%, TR 53%, HA 53%, SI 53%, DI 53%, PI 44%, IO 42% (lowest)
DNR achieves >=50% ASR in 82.8% of all model+architecture+defense configurations
Per-model ASR: Llama3-8B 70.8%, GPT-4o-mini 70.7%, Gemini-2.5-Flash 45.7%, DeepSeek-Reasoner 42.9% (cross-model range 42.9%-70.8%)
Cross-model stability (CV): DM 0.12, SNM 0.13, PM 0.16 (lowest/most stable)
Per-architecture ASR: Debate 88.5% (worst), Review 55%, Linear 49.5%, Vote 37.1% (most secure); DNR reaches 86.3% under Linear and 95% under Review
LLM-prompt defense ASR: DB 66.8% (worst), DC 63.4%, DO 53.7%, DA 46.3%

Traditional defense results: Google SafeBrowsing flags none (10 'No unsafe content found', 2 'No available data'); VirusTotal 0/98 for all variants except TI at 1/98 (a Seclookup 404-error false signal, not a real risk); PhishTank 'Nothing known' for all 12 variants
Each attack configuration repeated 10 times to compute average ASR
baselines_compared: Traditional URL defenses: Google SafeBrowsing, VirusTotal (98 detection engines), PhishTank

LLM-prompt defenses (adapted from prior work as defense baselines): Defense A (safety-prompt vigilance, Peigne et al. 2025), Defense B (HEXACO + Reid technique, Xie et al. 2025), Defense C (Sandwich / instruction-data separation, Liu et al. 2024), and Defense DO (authors' self-designed auditor prompt)

No prior ATTACK method is used as a quantitative baseline; WFA is reported in absolute ASR, with its weaker single-low-privilege-agent threat model contrasted qualitatively against prior multi/high-privilege-agent attacks
